events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old Hispanic woman named Marisol Fuentes-Delgado. She has a significant family history of breast and ovarian cancer (mother with breast cancer at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer). She is a former smoker (10 pack-years, quit 15 years prior), with a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin), hypertension (on lisinopril), and obesity (BMI 33.2). She is postmenopausal. ECOG performance status 1 at time of diagnosis.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of stage IIB (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition) CARCINOMA, DUCTAL, INFILTRATING (ICD-O histology code 8500/3) of the LEFT BREAST, UPPER OUTER QUADRANT (ICD-O site code C50.4). Relevant biomarkers included: Estrogen Receptor (ER): Negative (0% staining, Allred score 0); Progesterone Receptor (PR): Negative (0% staining, Allred score 0); HER2 by IHC: Negative (1+); HER2 by FISH: Not amplified (HER2/CEP17 ratio 1.2); Ki-67: 92%; BRCA1/2 germline testing: BRCA1 pathogenic variant detected (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74); PD-L1 CPS (22C3 assay): 28 (positive). Tumor grade: Histologic grade 3 (Nottingham grade: tubular formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic count 3). Tumor size: 3.4 cm by ultrasound. Lymph nodes: 1 clinically suspicious axillary lymph node on imaging confirmed by ultrasound-guided FNA. Bilateral breasts examined; no contralateral lesion. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) subtype, basal-like. Other relevant diagnostic information: Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via core needle biopsy; Laterality: Left; Lymphovascular invasion: Present; Androgen Receptor (AR): Negative (<1%); Germline genetic counseling completed prior to treatment initiation.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (core needle biopsy, left breast, 12 o'clock position, ultrasound-guided). The specimen showed invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham grade 9/9). Tumor cells were arranged in solid sheets and nests with central necrosis. Nuclei were markedly pleomorphic with prominent nucleoli. Mitotic rate was 28 per 10 HPF. No in-situ component identified on this core biopsy specimen. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative (0%, Allred 0), PR negative (0%, Allred 0), HER2 1+ (IHC), Ki-67 92%, CK5/6 positive (basal-like phenotype), EGFR positive (2+), p53 overexpression (strong diffuse, suggesting TP53 mutation), E-cadherin intact, AR negative. PD-L1 CPS 28 (22C3 assay). Pathologic diagnosis: Invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative, basal-like subtype.

At age 58 years, the patient had a DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAM AND BREAST ULTRASOUND (bilateral), which showed: Left breast - a 3.4 x 2.9 x 3.1 cm irregular, hyperdense mass at 12 o'clock position, 4 cm from nipple, with associated skin thickening but no skin invasion. Left axilla - one enlarged lymph node (1.8 cm, cortical thickening 8 mm, loss of fatty hilum) suspicious for metastatic involvement. Right breast - no suspicious lesion identified. ACR BI-RADS 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). Cancer status: Present. Metastatic sites: Left axillary lymph node suspicious.

At age 58 years, the patient had a STAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST, which showed: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses. Mediastinum: No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Liver: No hepatic lesions. Abdomen/pelvis: No suspicious lymphadenopathy or organ metastases. Left axilla: Enlarged left axillary lymph node as noted on prior ultrasound. Bones: No lytic or blastic lesions identified. Impression: No distant metastases. Consistent with locoregional disease only. Cancer status: Locoregional disease, no distant metastases.

At age 58 years, the patient had a BONE SCAN (technetium-99m), which showed: No evidence of osseous metastatic disease. Mild degenerative changes in the lumbar spine and bilateral knees (bilateral knee osteoarthritis), consistent with degenerative etiology. Cancer status: No osseous metastases.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for triple-negative breast cancer based on a specimen obtained from the primary left breast tumor (core needle biopsy). A 500-gene panel was used (FoundationOne CDx). Clinically actionable alterations: TP53 p.R248W (hotspot mutation, variant allele frequency 68%, pathogenic); BRCA1 c.5266dupC germline pathogenic variant confirmed in tumor (loss of heterozygosity at BRCA1 locus, consistent with biallelic inactivation); PIK3CA wild-type; PTEN loss (homozygous deletion); RB1 p.L153Rfs*11 (frameshift, loss of function); CDKN2A p.P114L (pathogenic); MYC amplification (copy number 18.4, high-level amplification). Additional somatic mutations (VUS or likely benign): NF1 p.D1644N (VUS); ARID1A p.Q330* (truncating, likely pathogenic); FAT3 p.S2152L (VUS); SPEN p.R2196H (VUS); RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (truncating, VUS); KDM6A p.R479C (VUS); ZFHX3 p.D1636N (VUS). Fusions: No clinically significant gene fusions detected. Copy number alterations: MYC amplification (8q24), PTEN homozygous deletion (10q23), CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion (9p21), RB1 deletion (13q14), MDM4 amplification (1q32). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Tumor mutational burden: 9 mut/Mb (intermediate). Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 62 (high, above threshold of 42). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (LOH): 24% genome-wide LOH. Mutational signature analysis: SBS3 (HRD/BRCA-associated) dominant signature. PD-L1 CPS 28 (from IHC, confirmed). No NTRK fusions detected. No PIK3CA hotspot mutations. No ESR1 mutations.

At age 58 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (cycle 1) concurrent with the initiation of dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV plus cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (ddAC, cycle 1 of 4). Filgrastim was prescribed for growth factor support given dose-dense schedule and patient's age. Metformin was held on days of chemotherapy per institutional protocol. Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, and fosaprepitant. Baseline LVEF by echocardiogram: 62%.

At age 58 years (week 3 of neoadjuvant therapy), the patient experienced grade 2 fatigue and grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to ddAC chemotherapy. She also developed grade 1 alopecia (total alopecia by cycle 2). Blood counts: ANC nadir of 0.8 x10^9/L (grade 3 neutropenia) after cycle 1 of ddAC, managed with filgrastim dose adjustment and no dose reduction required. Additionally, grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (fingertip tingling) noted, attributed to early taxane sensitization in setting of hyperglycemia; endocrinology consulted for improved glycemic control. No immune-related adverse events from pembrolizumab at this time. HbA1c was 8.4%, metformin dose increased.

At age 58 years (after cycle 2 of ddAC/pembrolizumab, approximately week 6), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Clinical breast exam showed a measurable decrease in palpable tumor size (estimated 2.0 cm vs. 3.4 cm at diagnosis). Left axillary lymph node no longer palpable. Patient tolerating therapy with manageable side effects. ECOG PS 1. Continued on protocol. Cancer present but responding to therapy. No new symptoms. Blood pressure elevated to 148/92 mmHg; lisinopril dose increased. Diabetes management ongoing with endocrinology co-management.

At age 58 years (mid-neoadjuvant, after cycle 4 of ddAC/pembrolizumab), the patient had a BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST, which showed: Left breast - residual enhancing mass measuring 1.6 x 1.4 cm (down from 3.4 cm at baseline; partial radiographic response, approximately 53% reduction). No new skin or chest wall involvement. Left axilla - previously suspicious lymph node now measures 1.0 cm with partially restored fatty hilum; may represent partial treatment response. No new suspicious lesions in either breast. No distant sites of disease. Cancer status: Present, partial response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Responding but residual disease remains.

At age 58 years (week 10, during transition to taxane phase), the patient developed grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism attributed to pembrolizumab. TSH 18.4 mU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL. Endocrinology consulted. Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated. Pembrolizumab continued without dose interruption per KEYNOTE-522 protocol guidelines. The patient also developed grade 2 arthralgia affecting bilateral knees and wrists (likely immune-related arthritis vs. worsening osteoarthritis); NSAIDs initiated (ibuprofen 400 mg TID with food). No elevated liver enzymes, no skin rash, no pneumonitis.

At age 58 years (approximately week 13 of neoadjuvant therapy), the patient transitioned to weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV for 12 weeks concurrent with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (cycles 5-8 of pembrolizumab). Carboplatin was NOT added to the paclitaxel phase given the patient's borderline renal function (CrCl 52 mL/min by CKD-EPI, attributed to longstanding hypertension and diabetes) and physician discretion to avoid cumulative nephrotoxicity and myelosuppression. Pre-medications included dexamethasone 20 mg, diphenhydramine 50 mg, and famotidine 20 mg prior to each paclitaxel infusion.

At age 58 years (week 18, during weekly paclitaxel/pembrolizumab), the patient developed grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (loss of sensation in hands and feet to mid-palm and mid-foot level, affecting fine motor tasks and gait). Neurology was consulted. Paclitaxel dose was reduced to 65 mg/m2 (approximately 20% reduction) and duloxetine 30 mg daily was initiated for neuropathic pain management. Patient declined further dose reduction. Grade 2 nail changes (onycholysis of bilateral great toes) also noted. No discontinuation of therapy. Pembrolizumab continued without modification.

At age 58 years (pre-surgery assessment, approximately week 26, after completion of all neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had an oncologist and breast surgery pre-operative office assessment. She completed 8 cycles of pembrolizumab (neoadjuvant phase), 4 cycles of ddAC, and 12 weeks of paclitaxel (dose-reduced for last 4 weeks). Clinical breast exam: small residual palpable firmness at prior tumor site (estimated < 1.5 cm, consistent with fibrosis vs. residual tumor). Left axilla: no palpable lymphadenopathy. ECOG PS 1. Peripheral neuropathy grade 2 at this assessment (improved from grade 3 with duloxetine and dose reduction). Repeat echocardiogram: LVEF 58% (down from 62% baseline; mild decline, no clinical heart failure). Cardiology notified; surveillance echocardiogram planned post-operatively. Surgery scheduling underway for left modified radical mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy. Cancer present (likely residual), not fully resolved. Patient counseled regarding germline BRCA1 status and option for contralateral prophylactic mastectomy (CPM), which she elected to undergo simultaneously.

At age 58 years (approximately month 7 post-diagnosis), the patient underwent LEFT MODIFIED RADICAL MASTECTOMY with immediate tissue expander placement for reconstruction (left side), CONTRALATERAL PROPHYLACTIC RIGHT MASTECTOMY with immediate tissue expander placement (right side), and SENTINEL LYMPH NODE BIOPSY of the left axilla (3 sentinel nodes identified). The procedure was performed under general anesthesia without intraoperative complications. Estimated blood loss: 280 mL. No intraoperative blood transfusion required. Wound closed primarily. A Jackson-Pratt drain was placed bilaterally. Immediate post-operative period was uneventful. Pathology specimen sent for full analysis.

At age 58 years (surgical specimen, post-neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (left modified radical mastectomy specimen and sentinel lymph nodes). Findings consistent with MIXED PATHOLOGIC RESPONSE (NOT a complete pathologic response; residual invasive disease present). Primary tumor bed: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.8 x 1.4 cm (largest dimension). Extensive treatment effect present with fibrosis, foamy macrophages, and hemosiderin deposition occupying approximately 60% of tumor bed area. Residual viable tumor cells present in approximately 40% of tumor bed. Lymphovascular invasion: Present (focal). Margins: All surgical margins negative (closest margin 5 mm). In-situ component: Residual high-grade DCIS present, 3 cm extent (ductal, cribriform pattern). Skin: No dermal involvement. Nipple: No Paget disease. Sentinel lymph nodes: 1 of 3 sentinel lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (micro-metastasis, 2.1 mm deposit, no extranodal extension). Right breast (CPM): No evidence of malignancy; fibrocystic changes only. Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) calculation: RCB index 2.4, classified as RCB class II (moderate residual disease). Immunohistochemistry on residual tumor: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative (confirms triple-negative phenotype maintained post-treatment). Ki-67 on residual tumor: 55% (decreased from pre-treatment 92%, indicating partial treatment effect). Pathologic stage: ypT1cN1mi (AJCC 8th edition), Stage IIA post-neoadjuvant.

At age 58 years (post-operative visit, approximately 3 weeks after surgery), the patient had an oncologist post-operative office assessment. Surgical wounds healing well bilaterally. Tissue expanders in place with planned exchange to permanent implants in approximately 6 months. Drain outputs minimal and drains removed. Pathology results reviewed with patient: residual invasive carcinoma (RCB-II), one positive sentinel lymph node (micro-metastasis). Discussed implications of residual disease and plan for adjuvant therapy per standard of care (CREATE-X/KEYNOTE-522 based: adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab continuation). Given germline BRCA1 mutation, discussed option of adjuvant olaparib (OlympiA trial data) - patient elected to forego olaparib at this time given peripheral neuropathy concerns and polypharmacy burden; option to be revisited. Referral to radiation oncology for post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) given positive lymph node. ECOG PS 1. Cancer: residual disease, not in complete remission. Follow-up imaging and labs ordered.

At age 58 years (beginning approximately 6 weeks post-surgery, after wound healing confirmed), the patient received POST-MASTECTOMY RADIATION THERAPY (PMRT) to the left chest wall and regional lymphatics (left axilla levels I-III, supraclavicular fossa, internal mammary nodes). Technique: 3D-conformal radiation therapy with tangential fields and supraclavicular field. Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) to the chest wall and regional nodes, with a boost of 10 Gy in 5 fractions to the prior tumor bed (total dose to tumor bed: 60.4 Gy). Concurrent pembrolizumab was held during radiation per institutional protocol (last pembrolizumab dose given 3 weeks prior to radiation start). Radiation completed over approximately 7 weeks. No significant acute toxicities during radiation; grade 1 skin erythema at radiation fields, managed with topical aquaphor. No pneumonitis during or immediately after radiation.

At age 58 years (approximately 3 weeks after completion of PMRT), the patient developed grade 2 radiation pneumonitis (new dry cough, mild dyspnea on exertion, low-grade fever 37.9°C). CT chest showed new ground-glass opacities in the left lung, predominantly in the radiation field. Pulmonology consulted. Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60 mg/day) initiated with planned taper over 8 weeks. Pembrolizumab restart was deferred pending resolution of pneumonitis. The patient also had grade 1-2 left breast/chest wall skin desquamation (moist at folds, managed with wound care). Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine (TSH 2.8 mU/L at this visit). Peripheral neuropathy improved to grade 1 (residual mild tingling in fingertips only).

At age 58 years (approximately 4.5 months post-surgery, after pneumonitis resolved and steroids fully tapered), the patient initiated ADJUVANT CAPECITABINE 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily on days 1-14 of a 21-day cycle (planned for 8 cycles, per CREATE-X protocol), along with PEMBROLIZUMAB 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (resuming adjuvant phase, per KEYNOTE-522 protocol, for approximately 9 remaining cycles to complete up to 1 year of total pembrolizumab). Patient counseled regarding hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, and mucositis risks with capecitabine. Baseline labs: CBC, CMP, renal function (CrCl 49 mL/min by CKD-EPI; capecitabine dose adjusted to 850 mg/m2 BID given CrCl < 51 mL/min to reduce renal toxicity risk). Thyroid function: normal on levothyroxine. HbA1c: 7.6% (improved with endocrinology co-management).

At age 58 years (cycle 2 of adjuvant capecitabine/pembrolizumab), the patient developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): erythema, peeling, and painful swelling of palms and soles, affecting daily activities. Capecitabine dose held for 7 days, then resumed at 750 mg/m2 BID (further 12% dose reduction from already-adjusted dose). Urea 40% cream applied topically three times daily. Grade 1 mucositis also present (mild oral soreness, no ulcerations). No grade 3/4 toxicities. Pembrolizumab continued at same dose. TSH rechecked: 4.1 mU/L (normal range). No new immune-related adverse events.

At age 59 years (approximately 6 months after surgery, during adjuvant therapy - cycle 4 of capecitabine/pembrolizumab), the patient had a SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST, which showed: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Lungs: Resolution of prior ground-glass opacities (radiation pneumonitis resolved). No new pulmonary nodules or masses. Chest wall: Post-surgical/post-radiation changes in left chest wall without evidence of local recurrence. No suspicious mediastinal or axillary lymphadenopathy. Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands: No suspicious lesions. No new findings. Cancer status: No evidence of disease (NED) on imaging. Responding to adjuvant therapy / no active disease identified.

At age 59 years (mid-adjuvant therapy, cycle 5 of capecitabine/pembrolizumab), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Patient tolerating reduced-dose capecitabine reasonably well; hand-foot syndrome improved to grade 1 with dose reduction and topical care. She completed 4 cycles of capecitabine. Pembrolizumab being administered concurrently. Surveillance CT NED. ECOG PS 1. Peripheral neuropathy remains grade 1 (stable, not worsening). Weight stable. Blood pressure well-controlled (132/80 mmHg on lisinopril). Glycemic control improved (HbA1c 7.2%). Patient reporting significant fatigue (grade 2, likely multifactorial: chemotherapy, hypothyroidism, diabetes, emotional distress) and moderate depression/anxiety related to diagnosis and treatment burden; referral to oncology social work and psychiatry initiated. Levothyroxine dose adjusted upward to 88 mcg based on TSH 5.6 mU/L. Cancer: no evidence of disease on imaging. Continues adjuvant therapy.

At age 59 years (approximately 14 months post-surgery, after completing adjuvant capecitabine and during final phase of pembrolizumab maintenance), the patient had a SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND BONE SCAN, which showed: CT findings - New 1.4 x 1.2 cm hypoattenuating lesion in the right hepatic lobe (segment VI), new compared to prior imaging. A second smaller 0.8 cm lesion in segment VII. No lymphadenopathy. No pulmonary lesions. Bone scan - New focal area of increased uptake in the right 8th rib posterolaterally, not identified on CT (lytic lesion on targeted CT rib series: 1.5 cm lytic lesion confirmed). Bilateral knee degenerative changes unchanged. Cancer status: SUSPICIOUS FOR METASTATIC DISEASE - new hepatic lesions and right rib lytic lesion consistent with distant metastatic recurrence. Urgent clinical correlation and tissue confirmation recommended.

At age 59 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (CT-guided core needle biopsy, right hepatic lobe lesion, segment VI). The specimen showed METASTATIC CARCINOMA consistent with breast primary. Tumor cells arranged in nests and cords with central necrosis. Nuclei markedly pleomorphic with prominent nucleoli. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative by IHC (1+), confirming triple-negative phenotype maintained at metastatic site. GATA3 positive (supports breast origin). CK7 positive, CK20 negative, TTF-1 negative, CDX2 negative (excludes lung and GI primary). Ki-67 78%. Diagnosis: Metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma, consistent with known primary. HER2 FISH not repeated at metastatic site at this time (IHC clearly negative).

At age 59 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer recurrence based on a specimen obtained from the right hepatic lobe metastasis (core needle biopsy). FoundationOne CDx 500-gene panel. Comparison to prior primary tumor NGS performed. Clinically actionable alterations (new or maintained): TP53 p.R248W (maintained, VAF 71%); BRCA1 biallelic inactivation maintained (germline c.5266dupC + somatic LOH); PIK3CA wild-type (unchanged); PTEN homozygous deletion (maintained); AKT1 p.E17K (NEW somatic mutation, VAF 28%, not present in primary tumor - potential acquired resistance mechanism); MYC amplification (maintained, copy number 22.1, further amplified); CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion (maintained); RB1 loss (maintained). New alterations compared to primary tumor: AKT1 p.E17K (acquired); CCND1 amplification (11q13, copy number 8.2, new - potential acquired resistance); RAD51C p.P34R (VUS, possible role in BRCA pathway). Loss of alterations: ARID1A p.Q330* not detected in metastatic specimen (possible clonal evolution). Fusions: No clinically significant fusions. MSS maintained. TMB: 11 mut/Mb (slight increase). HRD score: 68 (maintained high). Mutational signatures: SBS3 dominant (HRD) with new contribution of SBS17 (possible 5-FU exposure from capecitabine). No NTRK fusions. No FGFR amplification. No KRAS/NRAS mutations.

At age 59 years (upon confirmed diagnosis of metastatic TNBC recurrence, approximately 16 months post-surgery), pembrolizumab maintenance was discontinued. The patient was enrolled in a clinical trial for metastatic TNBC (BRCA1-mutated, platinum-naive). The patient received first-line metastatic TNBC therapy with SACITUZUMAB GOVITECAN-HZIY 10 mg/kg IV on days 1 and 8 of a 21-day cycle (Trodelvy, approved for unresectable or metastatic TNBC after ≥2 prior therapies; used here in context of clinical trial/expanded access as initial metastatic treatment given prior anthracycline, taxane, and capecitabine exposure) PLUS OLAPARIB 300 mg orally twice daily (given germline BRCA1 mutation and HRD-high status; per OlympiAD data and clinical trial combination). Note: physician elected combination given exhaustion of standard agents in neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting and BRCA1 mutation. UGT1A1 genotyping performed: patient is UGT1A1*28/*28 homozygous (increased risk of neutropenia and irinotecan/SN-38 toxicity); sacituzumab govitecan starting dose reduced to 7.5 mg/kg per label guidance for UGT1A1*28 homozygotes.

At age 59 years (cycle 2 of sacituzumab govitecan/olaparib for metastatic disease), the patient developed grade 4 neutropenia (ANC 0.1 x10^9/L) consistent with expected UGT1A1*28/*28-related severe toxicity. She was hospitalized for febrile neutropenia (temperature 38.8°C, ANC 0.08 x10^9/L) and treated with IV piperacillin-tazobactam and filgrastim G-CSF. Blood cultures: negative. CXR: no pneumonia. Duration of hospitalization: 4 days. Sacituzumab govitecan held for this cycle; dose subsequently reduced to 5 mg/kg at next cycle per protocol. Olaparib continued without interruption during hospitalization. Grade 1 diarrhea also present (2-3 loose stools per day), managed with loperamide. Grade 2 fatigue persisted. No new nausea/vomiting. Anemia grade 2 (Hgb 9.1 g/dL); erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not used per institutional policy in metastatic setting; transfusion threshold set at Hgb < 7.5 g/dL.

At age 59 years (after 3 cycles of sacituzumab govitecan/olaparib, approximately 3 months into first-line metastatic treatment), the patient had a RESTAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST, which showed: Right hepatic lesions: segment VI lesion decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.7 cm; segment VII lesion decreased from 0.8 cm to below threshold of reliable measurement (possible complete response at segment VII). No new hepatic lesions. No new pulmonary lesions or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Right 8th rib lytic lesion: stable in size (1.5 cm); no new osseous lesions. No new sites of metastatic disease. Impression: PARTIAL RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE to sacituzumab govitecan plus olaparib per RECIST 1.1 (target lesion sum decrease >30%). Cancer status: Present, partial response to first-line metastatic therapy. Responding to therapy.

At age 59 years (clinic visit after restaging imaging, approximately 4 months into metastatic treatment), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Partial response confirmed on CT imaging. Patient tolerated reduced-dose sacituzumab govitecan (5 mg/kg) with improved but persistent grade 1-2 neutropenia (ANC nadir 1.0-1.4 x10^9/L with prophylactic pegfilgrastim given on day 2 of each cycle). Olaparib ongoing at 300 mg BID with manageable nausea (grade 1-2). Fatigue grade 2 persists; functional status ECOG PS 1-2. Pain from right rib lesion managed with acetaminophen/ibuprofen (scheduled); patient declining opioids at this time. Quality of life discussion held; palliative care consultation initiated proactively for symptom management and goals-of-care planning. Patient expressed desire to continue active treatment with curative intent if possible; realistic discussion regarding metastatic disease prognosis held. Peripheral neuropathy grade 1 (stable from prior therapy). Hypothyroidism stable. Glycemic control HbA1c 7.8%. Cancer status: present, responding to first-line metastatic therapy. Overall disease course: metastatic recurrence approximately 14 months after surgery; on active treatment with partial response.

documents.text:
BILATERAL BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST (NEOADJUVANT TREATMENT RESPONSE ASSESSMENT)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Marisol Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic postmenopausal woman with recently diagnosed left breast triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, Stage IIB (T2N1M0) with known left axillary nodal involvement. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed (c.5266dupC). She is currently undergoing neoadjuvant chemotherapy with dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (ddAC) in combination with pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-522 protocol). This MRI is obtained for mid-treatment response assessment following completion of 4 cycles of ddAC and concurrent pembrolizumab. Baseline tumor size was 3.4 cm on diagnostic ultrasound with one suspicious left axillary lymph node. Prior staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan demonstrated no evidence of distant metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE:
Bilateral breast MRI performed at 1.5 Tesla using a dedicated bilateral breast coil. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T2-weighted fat-saturated images bilaterally, axial T1-weighted pre-contrast and dynamic post-contrast series (4 phases at 90-second intervals) following intravenous administration of 0.1 mmol/kg gadobutrol (Gadavist), subtraction images, and maximum intensity projection (MIP) reconstructions. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with ADC mapping performed bilaterally. CAD analysis performed. No adverse reaction to contrast material.

COMPARISON:
Diagnostic mammogram and targeted breast ultrasound from prior to initiation of therapy (index imaging). No prior breast MRI available for direct comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:

Primary Tumor Site:
In the upper central left breast at approximately the 12 o'clock position, roughly 4 cm posterior to the nipple, there is a residual area of irregular heterogeneous enhancement measuring approximately 1.6 x 1.4 x 1.5 cm in the axial and sagittal planes (previously measured at 3.4 x 2.9 x 3.1 cm on diagnostic ultrasound at initial presentation). This represents a substantial reduction from the baseline lesion -- in the range of approximately 50-55% linear reduction of the dominant dimension. The enhancement pattern demonstrates persistent irregular margins with heterogeneous internal enhancement. On kinetic analysis, the dominant kinetic curve type within the residual lesion demonstrates a mixed pattern with portions showing plateau kinetics and other areas with early washout, which may indicate a mixture of viable residual tumor and treatment-related fibrotic change. The T2-weighted signal within this region is mildly elevated and heterogeneous, consistent with admixed edema, fibrosis, and possible residual cellular tumor. On diffusion-weighted imaging, there remains a focus of restricted diffusion within the enhancing region (ADC approximately 0.82 x 10^-3 mm^2/s), slightly above the pre-treatment nadir (ADC values not available for direct comparison from baseline, but restricted diffusion persists), consistent with residual viable tumor.

There is no evidence of skin thickening or skin enhancement at the overlying dermis compared to the baseline examination. The pectoralis major muscle and the anterior chest wall show no involvement. No new enhancing satellite nodules are identified within the left breast. The previously noted peau d'orange type skin edema seen at the 12 o'clock position on baseline imaging is no longer appreciated.

There is a broad region of nonspecific background parenchymal enhancement (BPE) in the left breast at a low to moderate degree, which is expected in the post-treatment setting and somewhat complicates interpretation; however, the dominant residual lesion is still clearly delineated.

No new areas of suspicious enhancement are identified elsewhere in the left breast. No additional satellite lesions or multicentric foci of enhancement are identified in the left breast.

LEFT AXILLA:
In the left axillary region, a lymph node is identified measuring approximately 1.0 cm in short axis (previously reported as 1.8 cm with 8 mm cortical thickening and loss of fatty hilum on baseline ultrasound). The current appearance shows a partially reconstituted fatty hilum and less cortical thickening (now approximately 3-4 mm) compared to the baseline examination. The residual cortical thickening cannot be definitively attributed to treatment effect versus residual micrometastatic involvement on imaging alone, and remains indeterminate. No additional abnormal left axillary lymph nodes are identified. No suspicious level II or level III axillary lymph nodes.

LEFT INTERNAL MAMMARY REGION:
No pathologically enlarged or suspiciously enhancing lymph nodes are identified along the left internal mammary chain.

RIGHT BREAST:
No suspicious enhancement, mass, or architectural distortion identified in the right breast. No axillary lymphadenopathy on the right. Background parenchymal enhancement is symmetric and low. No suspicious lesion identified. The right breast appears entirely within normal limits for a postmenopausal patient of this age.

RIGHT AXILLA:
No abnormal lymph nodes identified in the right axilla.

SKIN AND NIPPLE-AREOLAR COMPLEX:
Left nipple-areolar complex: No retraction, enhancement, or involvement identified. Right nipple-areolar complex: Normal. Skin bilaterally: No focal skin thickening or enhancement.

CHEST WALL:
Bilateral pectoralis major and minor muscles appear intact. No chest wall involvement identified on either side.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. LEFT BREAST PRIMARY TUMOR -- PARTIAL TREATMENT RESPONSE: The dominant left breast lesion at the 12 o'clock position demonstrates a marked reduction in size from the pre-treatment baseline, with the residual enhancing component now measuring approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm (down from 3.4 cm at initial diagnosis by ultrasound). This represents an approximately 50-55% reduction in the dominant dimension, indicative of a meaningful partial response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy with ddAC and pembrolizumab. Residual irregular enhancement with mixed kinetics and persistent restricted diffusion suggests viable residual tumor is present within this region, though treatment-related fibrosis and stromal changes cannot be excluded without histologic correlation.

2. LEFT AXILLARY LYMPH NODE -- INDETERMINATE, POSSIBLY RESPONDING: The previously suspicious left axillary lymph node now measures approximately 1.0 cm with partial reconstitution of the fatty hilum. This morphologic evolution is consistent with treatment response, though radiographic normalization is incomplete. Residual nodal involvement cannot be excluded on imaging alone.

3. NO NEW SITES OF DISEASE: No new enhancing lesions in either breast, no new suspicious axillary or internal mammary adenopathy, and no chest wall involvement identified.

4. RIGHT BREAST: No suspicious findings. Normal postmenopausal appearance.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with an ongoing partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant systemic therapy. The residual enhancing mass with restricted diffusion warrants continued clinical and imaging follow-up. Definitive pathologic assessment of treatment response will be required at the time of surgical resection.

The background breast tissue shows mild-to-moderate fibrocystic changes including: stromal fibrosis, scattered microcysts and apocrine metaplasia, and mild ductal epithelial hyperplasia (usual type, without atypia). No atypical ductal hyperplasia (ADH) or atypical lobular hyperplasia (ALH) is identified in the background parenchyma. No additional synchronous carcinomas are identified.

SPECIMEN B - LEFT SENTINEL LYMPH NODES:

Intraoperative Assessment:
Frozen section and touch preparation cytology were performed intraoperatively on all three sentinel lymph nodes. Intraoperative result: Node 1 — atypical cells suspicious for micrometastasis (final deferred to permanent sections); Nodes 2 and 3 — negative for malignancy. [Results communicated to surgeon intraoperatively.]

Permanent Sections:
Node 1 (1.6 x 1.0 x 0.8 cm): Multiple serial step sections and cytokeratin immunohistochemistry (AE1/AE3 and CAM5.2) performed. A single discrete focus of metastatic carcinoma is identified in the subcapsular and peripheral sinuses of the lymph node, measuring 2.1 mm in greatest dimension. The deposit consists of clusters and individual malignant cells with high-grade nuclear features morphologically identical to the residual primary tumor. Extranodal extension is not identified; the metastatic deposit is entirely confined within the lymph node capsule. The surrounding lymph node parenchyma demonstrates partial effacement by fibrous tissue and sparse residual germinal center follicles, consistent with treatment effect. Designation: MICROMETASTASIS (pN1mi by AJCC 8th edition criteria; deposit > 0.2 mm and ≤ 2.0 mm... correction noted: deposit measures 2.1 mm, which technically meets criteria for macrometastasis [> 2.0 mm]; however, given the marginal measurement and borderline nature, see comment below).

Pathologist's Comment on Nodal Staging: The single focus of metastatic carcinoma in sentinel node 1 measures 2.1 mm at its greatest linear dimension. This measurement is at the boundary between micrometastasis (> 0.2 mm and ≤ 2.0 mm, pN1mi) and macrometastasis (> 2.0 mm, pN1a). Given the difficulty in distinguishing scattered cells from a cohesive deposit and the treatment-related distortion present, this finding is best classified as a micrometastasis (pN1mi) for clinical staging purposes, reflecting a deposit of 2.1 mm with borderline measurement. Treating clinicians should be aware of this borderline categorization.

Node 2 (1.1 x 0.7 x 0.6 cm): Serial sections and cytokeratin IHC reviewed. Negative for malignancy. Reactive changes with partial sinus histiocytosis. No isolated tumor cells identified.

Node 3 (0.9 x 0.7 x 0.4 cm): Serial sections reviewed. Negative for malignancy. Benign lymphoid tissue. No malignant cells on IHC.

Summary of Nodal Findings: 1 of 3 sentinel lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (micrometastasis/borderline, 2.1 mm, no extranodal extension). 2 of 3 sentinel lymph nodes negative.

Immunohistochemistry performed on residual invasive carcinoma from Specimen A (primary tumor bed, formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue from cassette A7, most representative viable tumor block):

This represents a marked decrease from the pre-treatment Ki-67 of 92% (assessed on prior core needle biopsy), indicating significant anti-proliferative treatment effect, though a high proliferative index persists in residual viable tumor.

RESIDUAL CANCER BURDEN (RCB) CALCULATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

RCB is calculated per the M.D. Anderson Cancer Center method (Symmans et al.):

- Primary tumor bed area: 2.2 x 1.8 cm (largest cross-sectional dimensions of tumor bed)
- Overall proportion of tumor bed that is invasive cancer: approximately 40% (cellularity estimate)
- Proportion of tumor that is in situ (DCIS): approximately 10% of residual viable cells are in situ
- Number of positive lymph nodes: 1
- Largest metastasis diameter: 0.21 cm (2.1 mm)

RCB index calculation:
- f_inv = 0.40; correction for in situ = (1 - 0.5 x 0.10) = 0.95; cellularity adjusted = 0.38
- d_primary = sqrt(2.2 x 1.8) = sqrt(3.96) = 1.99 cm
- Primary RCB contribution: 1.4 x (0.38 x 1.99)^0.17... [full mathematical derivation on file]
- Lymph node contribution: 1.0 positive node, largest metastasis 0.21 cm

Final RCB Index: 2.4
RCB Class: II (Moderate Residual Disease)

Interpretation: RCB-II is associated with an intermediate prognosis in TNBC following neoadjuvant therapy. The absence of pathologic complete response (pCR) in this patient with high pre-treatment Ki-67 (92%) and clinical stage IIB disease is clinically significant and supports consideration of escalated adjuvant therapy per current guidelines (e.g., CREATE-X protocol with cape; KEYNOTE-522 adjuvant Keytruda continuation).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Marisol Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic postmenopausal woman with a diagnosis of stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast, who presents today for her post-surgical oncology follow-up. She carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), which was identified at the time of diagnosis and factored heavily into her surgical decision-making.

By way of background, Ms. Fuentes-Delgado was initially diagnosed with a 3.4 cm grade 3 triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-) invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast with a single clinically suspicious ipsilateral axillary lymph node, confirmed by FNA. Her tumor was notable for a very high Ki-67 (92%), basal-like phenotype (CK5/6+, EGFR 2+), PD-L1 CPS of 28, and an HRD score of 62 on the FoundationOne CDx platform, consistent with her BRCA1 biallelic inactivation and SBS3-dominant mutational signature.

She received neoadjuvant therapy per the KEYNOTE-522 backbone: four cycles of dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (ddAC) followed by twelve weeks of weekly paclitaxel, all concurrent with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every three weeks. Carboplatin was intentionally omitted from the taxane phase due to borderline renal function (CrCl approximately 52 mL/min at the time of that decision), which has since trended slightly lower. Her neoadjuvant course was complicated by grade 3 neutropenia after cycle 1 of ddAC (managed with filgrastim dose adjustment), grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism attributed to pembrolizumab (levothyroxine initiated), and grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy during the paclitaxel phase (dose reduced from 80 to 65 mg/m2; duloxetine started). Notably, midpoint breast MRI after four cycles of ddAC/pembrolizumab showed the primary mass down to approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm from baseline 3.4 cm — a robust but incomplete radiographic response at that juncture.

Approximately three weeks ago, she underwent left modified radical mastectomy with immediate left tissue expander placement, contralateral prophylactic right mastectomy with immediate right tissue expander placement (elected in the context of her germline BRCA1 mutation after extensive counseling), and sentinel lymph node biopsy of the left axilla (3 nodes identified intraoperatively). She tolerated the procedure well; estimated blood loss was 280 mL with no intraoperative complications. Bilateral Jackson-Pratt drains were placed.

Today she returns for review of the final surgical pathology findings and to discuss the next phase of her care.

Current medications include: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, lisinopril (dose increased during neoadjuvant therapy for blood pressure management), metformin (dose adjusted during treatment; held on chemotherapy days per protocol), duloxetine 30 mg daily, ondansetron PRN nausea, and ibuprofen 400 mg TID (initiated for bilateral knee/wrist immune-related arthralgia during paclitaxel/pembrolizumab phase — patient reports this has largely resolved).

She denies fever, chills, wound drainage, or erythema at the surgical sites. She reports mild incisional tenderness bilaterally, improved from the immediate post-operative period. She continues to have mild tingling in her fingertips (residual neuropathy) and mild fatigue, both of which she says are tolerable and improving. She notes mild shortness of breath with exertion, which she attributes to deconditioning and surgical recovery, but this is new and warrants attention.

Final surgical pathology from the left modified radical mastectomy and sentinel lymph node biopsy has been reviewed in detail with the patient today. Key findings:

- Residual high-grade DCIS present, spanning 3 cm — also treated but incompletely eradicated.

PLAN

1. ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY — CAPECITABINE + PEMBROLIZUMAB CONTINUATION:
Given RCB class II residual disease following neoadjuvant therapy in a patient with TNBC, the standard-of-care approach per the CREATE-X and KEYNOTE-522 trial data is adjuvant capecitabine combined with continuation of pembrolizumab. We will plan for:
   - Capecitabine: Standard CREATE-X dosing is 1250 mg/m2 BID x 14 days q21 days x 8 cycles. Given her current renal function (CrCl approximately 47-49 mL/min — now below 51 mL/min), I am recommending a starting dose of 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 every 21 days with close renal monitoring. Renal function should be rechecked before cycle 1 and every 2 cycles. Dose will be adjusted accordingly. She will be counseled extensively regarding hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, mucositis, and the need to hold therapy at any grade 2 or higher skin toxicity.
   - Pembrolizumab: She received 8 doses during the neoadjuvant phase. She is eligible for up to approximately 9 additional doses to complete 1 year of total pembro per the KEYNOTE-522 protocol structure. Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks will resume concurrent with capecitabine, pending confirmation that no active immune-related adverse events are present at treatment initiation. Today's TSH is normal, and no active irAEs are identified.
   - Target start date: approximately 3-4 weeks from today, allowing continued surgical recovery and ensuring renal function has been reassessed.

2. ADJUVANT OLAPARIB — DISCUSSED BUT DEFERRED:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado has a germline BRCA1 mutation and HRD-high tumor, making her potentially eligible for adjuvant olaparib per the OlympiA trial (300 mg BID x 1 year), which demonstrated significant improvements in invasive disease-free survival in high-risk HER2-negative germline BRCA1/2-mutated breast cancer patients with residual disease or high pathologic stage. I reviewed this data with her in detail.

However, the patient expressed significant reluctance to add olaparib at this time. Her concerns center on polypharmacy burden (she is already on capecitabine, pembrolizumab, levothyroxine, metformin, lisinopril, and duloxetine), ongoing peripheral neuropathy that has not fully resolved, and overall treatment fatigue. I have documented her understanding of the potential benefit and her informed decision to forego olaparib at this time. I emphasized that this door remains open — olaparib could potentially be considered at a future juncture, particularly in the metastatic setting if recurrence occurs. We will revisit this decision at her 3-month follow-up and at completion of capecitabine.

3. POST-MASTECTOMY RADIATION THERAPY (PMRT) — REFERRAL PLACED:
Given the finding of a positive sentinel lymph node (micro-metastasis, 1 of 3 nodes), the patient meets criteria for PMRT to the left chest wall and regional lymphatics (including left axillary levels I-III, supraclavicular fossa, and internal mammary nodes) per ASCO/ASTRO guidelines. I have placed a referral to radiation oncology today. The radiation oncology team will complete their own consultation and simulation planning. PMRT should begin after adequate wound healing, typically 4-6 weeks post-surgery. Pembrolizumab will be held during radiation per our institutional protocol to avoid compounding radiation-related toxicity with immune activation.

4. CARDIAC SURVEILLANCE:
Echocardiogram showed LVEF of 57-58%, mildly decreased from pre-treatment baseline. Cardiology was notified. A repeat echocardiogram is scheduled in 3-4 months. No clinical heart failure at this time. Patient is asymptomatic from a cardiac standpoint (the mild dyspnea on exertion likely reflects deconditioning and post-surgical status rather than cardiac compromise, but will be monitored). Anthracycline cumulative dose was within standard limits; no further anthracyclines are planned. NSAIDs to be discontinued to reduce fluid retention risk and to protect renal function.

5. RENAL FUNCTION:
CrCl has trended from 52 to approximately 47-49 mL/min. Given sustained NSAID use and baseline diabetic nephropathy, I am discontinuing ibuprofen effective today. Patient counseled to use acetaminophen for pain/arthralgia as needed. Renal function to be rechecked in 4 weeks. Lisinopril to continue — it is renoprotective in the setting of diabetic nephropathy. BMP to be repeated at next visit.

6. NSAID DISCONTINUATION — see above. For residual arthralgia from prior immune-related joint symptoms, acetaminophen 500-1000 mg q6-8h PRN is appropriate. Ibuprofen is no longer appropriate given declining renal function.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM / ENDOCRINE:
TSH 3.4 mU/L — stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. Continue current dose. Thyroid function to be rechecked in 8 weeks or at next visit.

8. DIABETES:

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A is received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and "left breast 12 o'clock core biopsy." The container holds six tan-white to pale pink cylindrical core needle biopsy fragments, each measuring between 1.2 and 1.6 cm in length and approximately 1.5-2 mm in diameter. The cores are firm to rubbery in consistency with a homogeneous cut surface. No grossly hemorrhagic or necrotic areas are grossly appreciable, though several cores appear firm and slightly gritty on sectioning, raising suspicion for tumor. All six cores are entirely submitted in sequential cassettes (A1 through A3, two cores per cassette) and processed with standard overnight formalin fixation. Multiple levels obtained for histologic evaluation. Total tissue volume: approximately 0.9 cm3.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections reveal viable neoplastic tissue in five of six submitted cores. The tumor cells are arranged predominantly in solid sheets and cohesive nests with focal cord-like infiltration of the surrounding fibroadipose and fibrous stroma. A central geographic zone of comedo-type necrosis is identified within several of the solid aggregates. The stroma is desmoplastic with a moderate lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate at the periphery of the tumor nests, a finding consistent with the tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) pattern characteristic of some triple-negative carcinomas.

Nuclear features: The tumor nuclei are markedly enlarged and vesicular with coarse chromatin and strikingly prominent, often multiple nucleoli. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio is high. Nuclear pleomorphism is severe (nuclear pleomorphism score 3/3). Cytoplasm is moderate in amount, eosinophilic to amphophilic, occasionally with small lipid vacuoles.

Mitotic activity: Mitotic figures are numerous and are readily identified, including atypical forms (tripolar and irregular mitoses). Mitotic count: 28 mitotic figures per 10 high-power fields (HPF; 40x objective, field diameter 0.55 mm). Mitotic score 3/3.

Gland/tubule formation: No tubular differentiation is identified; tumor cells form exclusively solid nests and sheets (tubule formation score 3/3).

COMMENT:

This core needle biopsy from the left breast, 12 o'clock position, demonstrates invasive carcinoma of no special type (invasive ductal carcinoma), Nottingham histologic grade 3. The morphologic features — solid growth pattern with comedo-type necrosis, markedly pleomorphic nuclei, high mitotic rate (28/10 HPF), absent tubule formation, and desmoplastic stroma — are characteristic of a high-grade triple-negative breast carcinoma. The immunophenotype confirms the triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-) phenotype with a basal-like gene expression surrogate profile (CK5/6+, EGFR+, ER-, PR-, HER2-).

The aberrant p53 overexpression (strong diffuse pattern) is consistent with a TP53 hotspot gain-of-function mutation, which is encountered in approximately 70-80% of triple-negative/basal-like breast cancers. The markedly elevated Ki-67 index (92%) reflects very high proliferative activity and is characteristic of this aggressive subtype.

PD-L1 CPS of 28 by 22C3 assay is above the clinically relevant threshold (CPS >=10), which has been associated with benefit from immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) in both the neoadjuvant setting (KEYNOTE-522) and first-line metastatic setting (KEYNOTE-355).

Given the patient's strong family history and triple-negative breast cancer histology at age 58 (a presentation associated with BRCA1 germline pathogenic variants in approximately 10-20% of cases), germline genetic testing for BRCA1 and BRCA2 is strongly encouraged and has been initiated. Correlation with molecular/NGS testing on this specimen is also recommended per tumor board discussion.

No in-situ component is identified on this limited core biopsy material; assessment of the extent of DCIS and final surgical margin status must await the excision specimen. Lymphovascular invasion is suspected but cannot be definitively assessed on core biopsy.

These findings have been discussed with the treating oncology team. Tumor board conference review recommended prior to initiation of therapy.

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Ms. Marisol Fuentes-Delgado is a 59-year-old Hispanic woman with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) presenting for follow-up after restaging imaging showing partial response to ongoing first-line metastatic therapy with sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy) plus ola (Lynparza). **HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado carries a complex oncologic history that is reviewed briefly here for context. She was initially diagnosed with stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast at age 58, with a grade 3, Ki-67 92% tumor, lymphovascular invasion present, and a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC). Her tumor was also HRD-high (HRD score 62, SBS3 dominant mutational signature) and PD-L1 positive (CPS 28).

She received neoadjuvant pembro (Keytruda) plus dose-dense AC for 4 cycles followed by weekly pacli for 12 weeks (with Keytruda throughout), then underwent left modified radical mastectomy with bilateral prophylactic mastectomy given her BRCA1 mutation. Surgical pathology demonstrated a mixed pathologic response — residual invasive ductal carcinoma measuring 1.8 cm, one positive sentinel lymph node (micro-metastasis), and an RCB class II designation. She subsequently received post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) to the left chest wall and regional nodes (50.4 Gy with a 10 Gy boost), followed by adjuvant cape (dose-adjusted to 850 mg/m2 BID and subsequently reduced further to 750 mg/m2 BID due to grade 2 hand-foot syndrome) plus adjuvant Keytruda continuation.

Approximately 14 months post-surgery, surveillance imaging revealed new hepatic lesions (right hepatic lobe segments VI and VII) and a lytic right 8th rib lesion, confirmed by CT-guided liver biopsy showing metastatic TNBC (GATA3+, ER-/PR-/HER2- maintained). Given prior anthracycline (ddAC), taxane (pacli), and fluoropyrimidine (Xeloda) exposure in the neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting, and the patient's germline BRCA1 mutation with maintained HRD-high status, she was initiated on sacituzumab govitecan (SG) at a reduced starting dose of 7.5 mg/kg (then further reduced to 5 mg/kg after cycle 2 grade 4 febrile neutropenia hospitalization) plus Lynparza 300 mg orally twice daily as first-line metastatic therapy in the context of a clinical trial/expanded access framework.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION AND DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Microscopic examination of all three core biopsy fragments reveals sections of hepatic parenchyma with near-complete replacement by an infiltrating malignant epithelial neoplasm. The background liver parenchyma, where present, shows mild periportal fibrosis and mild steatosis, without significant hepatitis activity or cirrhosis. There is no bile duct proliferation or perisinusoidal fibrosis to suggest primary hepatic etiology.

Tumor Architecture and Cytology:
The neoplasm is composed of highly pleomorphic epithelioid cells arranged in irregularly anastomosing nests, solid sheets, and interconnecting cords separated by a desmoplastic fibrous stroma. Areas of central geographic necrosis are prominent, occupying approximately 30-35% of the tumor volume on the submitted sections. The tumor cells demonstrate markedly enlarged, hyperchromatic nuclei with coarse chromatin and one to multiple conspicuous nucleoli. The nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio is high. Cytoplasm is scant to moderate in amount, pale eosinophilic, and focally vacuolated. Cell borders are poorly defined. Mitotic figures are readily identified, averaging 18 per 10 high-power fields, including atypical mitoses. No squamous differentiation, glandular (tubular) differentiation, mucin production, or spindle cell component is identified. No intranuclear inclusions or bile production is seen, arguing against a primary hepatocellular carcinoma.

Stroma and Vascular Invasion:
The intervening stroma is densely fibrotic with scattered chronic inflammatory infiltrate including lymphocytes and plasma cells. Lymphovascular invasion is identified focally within the fragment (one focus of tumor cell cluster within a thin-walled vascular space). No perineural invasion is definitively identified on the limited biopsy material.

Adjacent Liver:
The non-neoplastic hepatic parenchyma at the biopsy edge demonstrates mild macrovesicular steatosis (estimated 10-15% of hepatocytes), mild lobular disarray, and mildly expanded portal tracts with fibrotic change, consistent with the patient's known history of type 2 diabetes and metabolic dysfunction. No viral cytopathic effect, bile plugs, or iron deposition is noted on routine staining.

SUMMARY OF GENOMIC FINDINGS

This specimen has been analyzed using the FoundationOne CDx assay, a Next Generation Sequencing (NGS)-based in vitro diagnostic device that uses targeted high-depth sequencing of 324 genes plus select intronic regions of 36 genes involved in rearrangements. Analysis detects substitutions, insertion-deletions, copy number alterations (amplifications and homozygous deletions), and rearrangements, as well as genomic signatures including tumor mutational burden (TMB) and microsatellite instability (MSI). This report has been augmented with full 500-gene panel data for comprehensive genomic profiling.

CLINICALLY RELEVANT GENOMIC ALTERATIONS (TIER I/II — ACTIONABLE OR POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE)

1. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   — Gene/Locus: TP53 (17p13.1)
   — Alteration Type: Missense substitution, hotspot mutation
   — Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 71% (compared to 68% in primary tumor; maintained)
   — Classification: Pathogenic (ClinVar, OncoKB Level 1 for loss-of-function context)
   — Functional Significance: Arginine-248 is the most frequently mutated codon in TP53 across all human cancers. The R248W substitution abolishes DNA contact with major groove binding, resulting in complete loss of transcriptional transactivation of canonical p53 targets (p21/CDKN1A, MDM2, PUMA, BAX). Additionally, this allele confers dominant-negative activity over wild-type p53 and gain-of-function oncogenic properties (enhanced invasion, drug resistance). This mutation was identified in the primary tumor and is maintained in the metastatic clone, consistent with early clonal driver status.
   — Therapeutic Relevance: No FDA-approved TP53-targeted therapies currently available in breast cancer; clinical trial eligibility may apply (APR-246/eprenetapopt for TP53 gain-of-function mutations; investigational).

   — Functional Significance: Loss of CDKN2A ablates both p16INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitor) and p14ARF (MDM2 inhibitor/TP53 stabilizer). Combined with TP53 mutation, this represents total disruption of both major G1/S cell cycle checkpoints and the ARF-MDM2-TP53 tumor suppressor pathway. CDKN2B loss further amplifies CDK4/6 activity.

- NF1 p.D1644N (VUS): Maintained from primary tumor. NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a RAS-GAP protein. This variant is of uncertain significance; if functional, could activate RAS/MAPK signaling. Tumor has no KRAS/NRAS mutations.
- KDM6A p.R479C (VUS): Maintained. KDM6A is a histone H3K27 demethylase; loss may alter chromatin state. Uncertain therapeutic significance.
- ZFHX3 p.D1636N (VUS): Maintained. Transcription factor; uncertain significance in breast cancer.
- RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (truncating, VUS): Maintained. RNF43 is a negative regulator of Wnt signaling; truncating mutations may activate Wnt pathway; porcupine inhibitors could be of interest if Wnt dependency confirmed.
- SPEN p.R2196H (VUS): Maintained. SPEN (SHARP) is involved in transcriptional repression and has been implicated in ER signaling modulation (not relevant given ER-negative phenotype).
- FAT3 p.S2152L (VUS): Maintained. FAT3 is an atypical cadherin; uncertain significance.
- RAD51C p.P34R (VUS): NEW in metastatic specimen (not detected in primary tumor). RAD51C is a paralogue of RAD51, essential for homologous recombination. This variant of uncertain significance could potentially affect HRR pathway function; classification as likely benign versus pathogenic pending functional studies. No impact on HRD score calculation.

BRCA REVERSION MUTATION ANALYSIS

High-depth analysis of BRCA1 exon 11 (c.5266 locus) was performed specifically to detect potential reversion mutations that restore BRCA1 reading frame — a well-characterized mechanism of PARP inhibitor and platinum resistance.
— RESULT: NO BRCA1 REVERSION MUTATIONS DETECTED at or surrounding c.5266dupC at this sequencing depth.
— Interpretation: PARP inhibitor resistance via BRCA1 reversion is not the mechanism operative at this time point. The AKT1 p.E17K and CCND1 amplification are more likely contributors to any emerging resistance.

TECHNICAL NOTES AND ASSAY LIMITATIONS

— Assay: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Inc.), CLIA-certified, CAP-accredited, FDA-approved for solid tumor genomic profiling
— Sequencing platform: Illumina HiSeq (hybrid capture, paired-end)
— Panel coverage: 324 cancer-related genes (full exonic coverage); 36 genes with intron coverage for fusion detection; whole-genome copy number analysis; TMB and MSI calculation from sequence data
— This report includes extended 500-gene panel data for research purposes
— Sensitivity: Single nucleotide variants and small insertions/deletions ≥5% allele frequency in adequate tumor content specimens; copy number amplifications ≥6 copies; homozygous deletions ≥1 exon; fusions in select gene pairs
— Germline contamination: This is a tumor-only assay; germline variants (e.g., BRCA1 c.5266dupC) may be detected but cannot be definitively classified as somatic or germline without a paired normal specimen. Germline status confirmed by prior dedicated germline testing.
— Limitations: This assay does not detect all genomic alterations. Certain large structural variants, epigenetic alterations, and non-coding mutations are not captured. Tumor heterogeneity may result in subclonal variants below detection threshold.
— All alterations are interpreted in the context of known cancer biology, published literature, and curated databases (OncoKB, ClinVar, COSMIC, CIViC).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is a 59-year-old Hispanic woman with a history of stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative, grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast diagnosed approximately 21 months ago. Her cancer is notable for germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), high HRD score (62), PTEN loss, MYC amplification, and PD-L1 CPS 28. She underwent neoadjuvant Keytruda plus dose-dense AC followed by dose-reduced weekly PTX (reduced for grade 3 peripheral neuropathy), then left modified radical mastectomy with contralateral prophylactic mastectomy. Surgical pathology showed mixed pathologic response with residual invasive carcinoma (RCB-II, ypT1cN1mi), prompting adjuvant cape (dose-reduced given CrCl and hand-foot syndrome) plus pembro per KEYNOTE-522 framework. She completed 8 cycles of Xeloda and was on Keytruda maintenance when surveillance imaging approximately 3 months ago identified new hepatic lesions (right lobe, segments VI and VII) and a right posterior 8th rib lytic lesion, biopsy-confirmed as metastatic TNBC. Repeat NGS on the metastatic biopsy demonstrated a new acquired AKT1 p.E17K mutation and CCND1 amplification compared to the primary, with maintained BRCA1 biallelic inactivation, HRD-high profile, and PTEN loss.

Given prior anthracycline, taxane, and cape exposure in the neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings, along with germline BRCA1 mutation and HRD-high status, she was initiated on sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy) plus Lynparza (Lynparza) as first-line metastatic therapy, enrolled through a clinical trial. UGT1A1 genotyping revealed *28/*28 homozygous status, and the sacituzumab govitecan dose was proactively reduced to 7.5 mg/kg. Despite this, she developed grade 4 febrile neutropenia during cycle 2, requiring a 4-day hospitalization with IV antibiotics (pip-tazo) and G-CSF support; cultures were negative. Sacituzumab govitecan was further reduced to 5 mg/kg per protocol. She has now completed 3 cycles of sacituzumab govitecan/Lynparza and presents today to review restaging imaging and assess toxicity and overall status.

She reports that she is functional at home but notes significant ongoing fatigue that she describes as limiting her ability to perform household tasks and reducing her quality of time with family. She denies fever, chills, or rigors. She reports continued right-sided rib pain, rated 3-4/10 at rest and up to 6/10 with certain movements (reaching, lifting). She is using acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours and ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily around the clock; this provides partial relief. She continues to decline opioid analgesics at this time, citing fear of sedation and constipation. She has mild intermittent loose stools (1-2 per day, grade 1) that she manages with dietary modification and occasional loperamide as needed. No nausea or vomiting. NUCLEAR MEDICINE BONE SCINTIGRAPHY
Technetium-99m Methylene Diphosphonate (MDP) Whole-Body Bone Scan

INDICATION:
58-year-old Hispanic postmenopausal woman with newly diagnosed stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast (grade 3, Ki-67 92%, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant). Referred for osseous staging evaluation prior to initiation of neoadjuvant systemic therapy. Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed separately (no lytic or blastic lesions identified on CT at time of referral).

TECHNIQUE:
Intravenous administration of 25.0 mCi (925 MBq) of technetium-99m MDP. Whole-body planar scintigraphy performed at approximately 3 hours post-injection in anterior and posterior projections using a dual-headed gamma camera. Additional SPECT/CT acquisition of the thoracolumbar spine and pelvis was performed to improve lesion characterization in regions of potential ambiguity. Selected spot views of the lumbar spine, pelvis, and bilateral knees were also obtained. No prior bone scans available for comparison. Recent staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis was reviewed in conjunction with this study.

FINDINGS:

SKULL AND FACIAL BONES:
Uniform, symmetric radiotracer uptake throughout the calvarium. No focal areas of abnormal increased or decreased uptake. No evidence of lytic destruction or sclerotic lesions.

CERVICAL AND THORACIC SPINE:
Physiologic distribution of radiotracer throughout the cervical vertebral bodies and posterior elements. Mild, heterogeneous uptake at multiple thoracic vertebral endplates, most prominent at T8-T10, consistent with degenerative endplate changes or mild spondylosis. These foci demonstrate corresponding degenerative changes on the staging CT and are not morphologically suspicious for osseous metastatic involvement. No photopenic defects or focal areas of dramatically increased uptake to suggest replacement of marrow by tumor.

LUMBAR SPINE:
Moderately increased radiotracer uptake noted diffusely throughout the lumbar vertebral bodies and facet joints, particularly at L3-L4 and L4-L5 levels bilaterally. SPECT/CT correlation confirms these correspond to facet arthropathy and degenerative disc disease with osteophyte formation, without associated cortical destruction or soft tissue mass. No evidence of pathologic vertebral involvement. There is no collapse deformity or epidural extension to suggest metastatic disease.

STERNUM AND RIBS:
Ribcage demonstrates symmetric radiotracer uptake bilaterally. No focal areas of abnormal increased or photopenic uptake are identified in any rib. No evidence of cortical disruption on correlative CT to suggest lytic metastatic lesions. Sternum: normal radiotracer distribution without focal abnormality.

SHOULDERS AND UPPER EXTREMITIES:
Symmetric radiotracer uptake in bilateral glenohumeral joints, acromioclavicular joints, and humeral diaphyses. Mild degenerative changes at bilateral acromioclavicular joints (symmetric, no focal metastatic lesion). No abnormal uptake in the forearms, wrists, or hands.

PELVIS AND SACRUM:
Mild, symmetric increased uptake at bilateral sacroiliac joints and along the iliac crests, consistent with age-appropriate degenerative change. The acetabular regions demonstrate normal physiologic uptake bilaterally. No focal areas of abnormal increased uptake within the iliac wings, pubic rami, ischii, or femoral heads to suggest metastatic involvement. Sacrum shows uniform, symmetric radiotracer distribution.

HIPS AND LOWER EXTREMITIES:
Symmetric radiotracer uptake throughout the proximal femora and femoral diaphyses bilaterally. No periosteal reaction or cortical irregularity. Mild increased uptake at bilateral knee joint spaces, predominantly at the medial compartments of both knees and the patellofemoral joints; SPECT/CT demonstrates corresponding joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis, entirely consistent with bilateral medial compartment osteoarthritis. No evidence of aggressive or permeative osseous lesion. Tibiae, fibulae, and feet demonstrate normal radiotracer distribution without focal abnormality.

SOFT TISSUES:
Blood pool images at the time of injection demonstrated no abnormal soft tissue hyperemia. Retained radiotracer in the renal collecting systems and bladder is physiologic. No abnormal extraskeletal radiotracer uptake is identified.

IMPRESSION:
1. NO EVIDENCE OF OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE. The whole-body bone scan is negative for pathologic skeletal involvement throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton.

2. Multifocal degenerative changes identified, as detailed above, most notably:
   a. Lumbar spondylosis and facet arthropathy at L3-4 and L4-5, confirmed as degenerative on SPECT/CT.
   b. Mild thoracic spondylosis at T8-T10 endplates.
   c. Bilateral knee osteoarthritis (medial compartment predominant) — these findings correlate with the patient's reported history and are consistent with degenerative etiology only.

3. The staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis reviewed in conjunction with this study likewise demonstrated no osseous lesions. Taken together, there is no scintigraphic or cross-sectional imaging evidence of skeletal metastatic spread at the time of initial staging.

4. Findings are consistent with locoregional disease (stage IIB) without distant osseous involvement. Clinical staging of M0 is supported.

GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY

SECTION 1: CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS (Tier I / Tier II)

1. TP53 p.R248W (Missense Substitution)
   - Gene: TP53 (17p13.1)
   - Alteration Type: Somatic point mutation; hotspot missense
   - Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 68%
   - Predicted Effect: Dominant-negative gain-of-function; loss of wild-type p53 tumor suppressor activity
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic. TP53 p.R248W is one of the most frequent hotspot mutations across human cancers and is among the most recurrent alterations in triple-negative breast cancer. The high VAF (68%) is consistent with heterozygous mutation in a tumor with high cellularity, or possible concurrent loss of the wild-type allele. Strong diffuse p53 immunohistochemical overexpression noted on prior pathology is concordant with this dominant-negative missense mutation. TP53 mutations of this type are associated with aggressive tumor biology, impaired apoptosis, and resistance to certain DNA-damaging agents. No currently approved targeted therapies are directed at TP53 p.R248W specifically; however, this alteration may have implications for prognosis and response to DNA-damaging chemotherapy agents.
   - Tier: IA (well-established pathogenic driver in breast cancer context)

2. BRCA1 Biallelic Inactivation (Germline + Somatic LOH)
   - Gene: BRCA1 (17q21.31)
   - Alteration Type: Germline pathogenic frameshift (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74) confirmed in tumor; somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus detected
   - Germline Variant Classification: Pathogenic (ClinVar, BIC database, ENIGMA consortium classification)
   - Somatic Second Hit: Loss of heterozygosity confirmed, consistent with biallelic inactivation of BRCA1 in the tumor
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; ACTIONABLE. Biallelic BRCA1 inactivation results in complete loss of homologous recombination repair (HRR) capacity in this tumor. This finding is the primary driver of the markedly elevated HRD score (see Section 4) and is directly therapeutically actionable: BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative breast cancer (including TNBC) is an FDA-approved indication for the PARP inhibitor ola (Lynparza; OlympiAD trial) and tala (Talzenna; EMBRACA trial) in the metastatic setting. In the neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting, OlympiA trial data support adjuvant ola for high-risk, HER2-negative, germline BRCA1/2-mutated early breast cancer. Platinum-based chemotherapy agents (Platinol, carbo) also exploit HRR deficiency and demonstrate enhanced activity in BRCA1-mutated TNBC.
   - Companion Diagnostic Status: BRCA1 pathogenic variant; positive for Lynparza eligibility (germline BRCA1/2 mutation in HER2-negative breast cancer) per FDA-approved CDx indication.
   - Tier: IA

3. PTEN Homozygous Deletion
   - Gene: PTEN (10q23.31)
   - Alteration Type: Homozygous deletion (both alleles lost); copy number = 0
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; loss of function. PTEN is a critical negative regulator of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. Homozygous deletion results in complete loss of PTEN protein expression, leading to constitutive PI3K pathway activation. PTEN loss is present in approximately 20-35% of TNBC and is associated with worse prognosis and potential resistance to certain therapies. PTEN loss may activate the PI3K/AKT pathway in lieu of PIK3CA mutation (see Section 3: PIK3CA wild-type). PTEN-deficient tumors may retain sensitivity to PI3K pathway inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, though not FDA-approved in TNBC), AKT inhibitors, or mTOR inhibitors in the appropriate context. This finding is consistent with the basal-like TNBC subtype.
   - Tier: IB

4. RB1 p.L153Rfs*11 (Frameshift Deletion)
   - Gene: RB1 (13q14.2)
   - Alteration Type: Somatic frameshift mutation; predicted loss of function
   - VAF: 44%
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; loss of function. RB1 encodes the retinoblastoma protein, a master regulator of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint. Loss-of-function RB1 mutations are found in approximately 20-25% of TNBC, particularly basal-like subtype. RB1 loss is associated with a high proliferative index (consistent with Ki-67 92% observed in this tumor) and may predict sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors paradoxically in some contexts (though CDK4/6 inhibitors generally require intact RB1 for efficacy in luminal breast cancer; in TNBC with RB1 loss, the biology is distinct). Also associated with increased sensitivity to DNA-damaging agents.
   - Tier: IB

5. CDKN2A p.P114L (Pathogenic Missense)
   - Gene: CDKN2A (9p21.3); also known as p16/INK4A
   - Alteration Type: Somatic pathogenic missense mutation
   - Additional Finding: CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion also detected at the 9p21.3 locus (see Copy Number Alterations section)
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; loss of function. CDKN2A encodes the p16 cell cycle inhibitor, which normally inhibits CDK4/6. Combined somatic point mutation and co-deletion effectively eliminates p16 activity, further dysregulating cell cycle progression. This co-occurs with RB1 loss in a tumor with complete loss of canonical cell cycle control at multiple nodes. CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion is a recurrent finding in aggressive basal-like TNBC.
   - Tier: IB

6. MYC Amplification (High-Level)
   - Gene: MYC (8q24.21)
   - Alteration Type: High-level amplification
   - Copy Number: 18.4 (markedly elevated; normal diploid = 2)
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; gain of function. MYC is a transcription factor and proto-oncogene. High-level MYC amplification is found in approximately 15-20% of TNBC and is associated with aggressive tumor phenotype, high Ki-67, and poor prognosis. MYC amplification may contribute to resistance to certain therapies but also represents a potential synthetic lethality vulnerability (e.g., CDK1 inhibition, BET bromodomain inhibitors targeting MYC transcription). No direct FDA-approved MYC-targeted therapy currently available, but this alteration is clinically significant for prognostication and emerging trial eligibility.
   - Tier: IB

The following somatic alterations were identified and are listed with their current classification. Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) are reported for informational purposes; clinical decision-making should not be based solely on VUS findings.

   - Context: NF1 encodes a RAS-GAP protein; loss-of-function NF1 mutations activate RAS/MAPK signaling. This specific missense variant has limited functional data.
