SECTION 5: MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) AND TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)

Microsatellite Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
- Assessment based on analysis of 95 intronic homopolymer and dinucleotide repeat loci.
- No evidence of mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) by genomic profiling.
- Clinical implication: Keytruda (Keytruda) and other anti-PD-1/PD-L1 agents are NOT indicated solely on the basis of MSI/dMMR in this patient (tumor is MSS). However, PD-L1 CPS of 28 (by 22C3 immunohistochemistry) independently supports pembro use in the TNBC context per KEYNOTE-522 (neoadjuvant) and KEYNOTE-355 (metastatic) trial data and current FDA approvals.

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 9 mutations per megabase (mut/Mb)
- Classification: Intermediate TMB (TMB-Low defined as <6 mut/Mb; TMB-High defined as >=10 mut/Mb per FDA threshold)
- Clinical Implication: This value does not meet the FDA TMB-High threshold (10 mut/Mb) that supports Keytruda use based on TMB alone (per Keytruda pan-tumor TMB-H indication). TMB at 9 mut/Mb is near the threshold; however, current evidence does not support clinical decision-making based on borderline TMB values in breast cancer specifically. The intermediate TMB in this BRCA1-deficient tumor is notable, as BRCA1-mutated TNBC can have variable TMB, and the HRD-associated mutational patterns (see Section 7) are more clinically relevant than TMB in this context.

---

SECTION 6: HOMOLOGOUS RECOMBINATION DEFICIENCY (HRD)

HRD Score: 62 (HIGH; above validated threshold of 42)
Genomic LOH (gLOH): 24% genome-wide loss of heterozygosity
Mutational Signature: SBS3 dominant (see Section 7)

HRD Methodology: The HRD score is a composite genomic scar metric derived from three components:
1. Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH) index
2. Telomeric Allelic Imbalance (TAI) index
3. Large-scale State Transitions (LST) index

Each component reflects the cumulative genomic scarring caused by prior deficiency in homologous recombination repair. An HRD score >=42 is considered HRD-positive by validated thresholds (Myriad myChoice CDx methodology; FoundationOne CDx uses concordant methodology).

Clinical Significance: HRD score of 62, combined with confirmed biallelic BRCA1 inactivation and dominant SBS3 mutational signature, establishes this tumor as definitively homologous recombination-deficient. This has the following clinical implications:
- PARP Inhibitor Sensitivity: Strong predictor of response to PARP inhibitors (ola, Talzenna, Zejula, ruca). Lynparza (Lynparza) is FDA-approved for germline BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative advanced breast cancer.
- Platinum Sensitivity: HRD-high status, particularly driven by BRCA1 loss, predicts enhanced sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy (cis, Paraplatin). This is particularly relevant in the neoadjuvant setting (e.g., BRCA-BRCAness subgroup of GeparSixto, BrighTNess trial).
- Neoadjuvant Immunotherapy Context: KEYNOTE-522 supports Keytruda addition to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in early TNBC regardless of HRD status, but the HRD-high profile supports anticipated tumor immunogenicity and treatment response.

gLOH of 24%: Consistent with marked genomic instability secondary to BRCA1-mediated HRR deficiency. High gLOH is an independent predictor of platinum and PARP inhibitor benefit.

---

SECTION 7: MUTATIONAL SIGNATURE ANALYSIS

Dominant Signature: SBS3 (COSMIC v3.3)
- SBS3 is the canonical signature associated with defective homologous recombination repair (BRCA1/2 dysfunction). It is characterized by an elevated burden of small insertions and deletions at microhomology junctions and a broad pattern of base substitutions. The dominance of SBS3 in this tumor is highly consistent with the confirmed BRCA1 biallelic inactivation.

Secondary Signatures:
- SBS5 (clock-like, aging-associated): Minor contribution. Expected background signature in a 58-year-old patient.
- SBS13 (APOBEC-associated; C>G at TCN context): Minor contribution detected. APOBEC activity is commonly upregulated in breast cancers with TP53 mutations and genomic instability.

Signature Interpretation: The mutational signature profile of this tumor is dominated by HRD-associated mutagenesis (SBS3), strongly consistent with the underlying BRCA1 germline and somatic inactivation. No tobacco-associated signatures (SBS4) identified, consistent with remote prior smoking history and absence of APOBEC/tobacco-driven biology as a major driver. No signatures associated with prior therapeutic exposures identified (no SBS17 at this time point).

This tumor-only comprehensive genomic profiling assay confirms the previously identified germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74) in the tumor specimen context, with somatic LOH confirming biallelic inactivation. This report is not a substitute for germline genetic testing or formal genetic counseling. Germline BRCA1/2 testing has been previously performed and genetic counseling has been completed per clinical documentation. First-degree relatives of the patient should be offered genetic counseling and cascade testing given the confirmed pathogenic BRCA1 germline variant.

Notably, the previously palpable and FNA-confirmed left axillary lymph node is no longer palpable today. While we cannot establish pathologic response based on clinical exam alone, this is a very promising early clinical sign. She is responding in both the primary and nodal compartments. I am cautiously optimistic. We will continue on protocol. Formal radiologic reassessment with breast MRI is planned after completion of cycle 4 of ddAC, as per the KEYNOTE-522 schema. If she achieves a complete clinical and radiologic response by that point, we will continue to planned surgery, but the pathology at the time of resection will ultimately determine the need for adjuvant intensification.
   - Plan: Continue current regimen per protocol; mid-neoadjuvant MRI after cycle 4 ddAC.

2. TREATMENT - CYCLE 3 ddAC/pembro:
The patient is cleared to proceed with cycle 3 of dose-dense Adriamycin 60 mg/m2 IV plus Cytoxan 600 mg/m2 IV today (no dose reduction required based on current counts and organ function). pembro 200 mg IV will be administered concurrently. Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, fosaprepitant per standard protocol. Filgrastim 480 mcg SC to be initiated 24-48 hours post-chemotherapy and continued for 7 days. Metformin held on chemotherapy administration days per protocol.
   - Cumulative anthracycline dose: 120 mg/m2 after this cycle (total planned 240 mg/m2 over 4 cycles of ddAC; within acceptable limits)
   - Baseline echocardiogram (LVEF 62%) documented; cardiac monitoring to be repeated mid-treatment per institutional protocol
   - Plan: Administer cycle 3 as above.

3. HEMATOLOGIC TOXICITY (Prior Grade 3 Neutropenia):
ANC recovered appropriately to 3.9 x10^9/L today with filgrastim support. No dose reduction required at this time. Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC nadir 0.8 x10^9/L) after cycle 1 was expected given dose-dense schedule, particularly in the setting of borderline renal function; filgrastim protocol was optimized. No febrile neutropenia has occurred to date, which is reassuring. Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL reflects mild anemia - will monitor; no transfusion indicated at this time.
   - Plan: Continue filgrastim per protocol. Monitor CBC at each cycle. Threshold for considering pegfilgrastim substitution or further dose adjustment if febrile neutropenia occurs.

4. NAUSEA/VOMITING (Grade 2, Improving):
Nausea significantly improved after cycle 2 compared to cycle 1, suggesting antiemetic regimen optimization with fosaprepitant, ondansetron, and dexamethasone is effective. Patient using prochlorperazine breakthrough PRN with good results.
   - Plan: Continue current antiemetic protocol. Reinforce taking scheduled antiemetics for the first 3-5 days post-infusion (acute and delayed nausea). Small, frequent meals encouraged. Maintain hydration.

5. FATIGUE (Grade 2):
Multifactorial fatigue in the setting of chemotherapy, mild anemia, suboptimally controlled hyperglycemia, and emotional distress. Grade 2 fatigue is common during ddAC therapy and at this point does not warrant dose modification.
   - Plan: Encourage light physical activity as tolerated (walking 10-20 minutes daily if possible). Optimize glycemic control (see below). Iron studies to rule out iron deficiency contributing to anemia ordered. Address psychological component (see item 9).

6. HYPERTENSION:
Blood pressure elevated today at 148/92 mmHg, with patient reporting home readings consistently in the 150s/90s. This represents suboptimal blood pressure control in the context of existing cardiovascular risk and concurrent Adriamycin therapy (cardioprotection important). Lisinopril was recently increased; if not adequately controlling BP in next 2-3 weeks, will consider adding amlodipine.
   - Plan: Increase lisinopril dose from 10 mg to 20 mg daily. Recheck BP at cycle 3 visit (in 2 weeks). Patient counseled on low-sodium diet. Home BP monitoring log to be reviewed. Avoid NSAIDs (can raise BP and worsen renal function). Cardiology referral considered if BP remains uncontrolled.

7. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
HbA1c 8.4%, elevated. Improved glycemic control is important to reduce neuropathy risk and optimize overall treatment tolerance. Endocrinology has been consulted and is co-managing. Metformin was recently uptitrated.
   - Plan: Continue metformin at increased dose. Defer to endocrinology for additional medication adjustments. Hold metformin on chemotherapy days as per protocol. Fasting glucose checks at home encouraged. Low glycemic index diet counseling reinforced.

8. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (Grade 1):
Bilateral fingertip tingling, unchanged from prior assessment. This likely reflects early chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy amplified by suboptimal glycemic control. Grade 1 is manageable and does not require dose modification at this time. Of note, Taxol will be introduced in the taxane phase (post-ddAC); we must monitor neuropathy carefully as this will likely worsen when taxane is added, particularly in a patient with pre-existing hyperglycemia-associated neuropathic predisposition.
   - Plan: Continue pyridoxine supplementation. Optimize glucose control. Monitor neuropathy closely at each visit. If grade 2 or higher neuropathy develops prior to taxane phase, will address proactively. Duloxetine to be considered if neuropathic pain develops.

9. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENTS (irAE) SURVEILLANCE:
No evidence of irAEs at this time. TSH and free T4 are normal (no thyroiditis). LFTs normal (no immune hepatitis). No rash, no diarrhea, no respiratory symptoms. PD-L1 CPS 28, which reflects a tumor with significant immune infiltration and should predict good response to pembro, though also potentially higher irAE risk.
   - Plan: Continue comprehensive irAE surveillance at each visit. Counsel patient regarding symptoms of irAEs - particularly thyroid dysfunction (fatigue, weight changes, palpitations), colitis (diarrhea, abdominal cramping), pneumonitis (cough, dyspnea), and rash. Repeat thyroid function at next cycle.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL DISTRESS:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is visibly distressed regarding her diagnosis, her hair loss, and uncertainty about her future. She expressed significant anxiety and fear today. This is a newly diagnosed patient with a high-risk breast cancer in the neoadjuvant setting, and emotional distress at this stage is expected and validated. She has strong family support.
    - Plan: Referral placed to oncology social work for psychosocial support and resource navigation. Discussed patient support groups for breast cancer / TNBC. Provided educational materials on BRCA1 and hereditary cancer in the family (reiterated referral for cascade testing for adult children). The body image impact of mastectomy (she is informed this is likely forthcoming) and alopecia were acknowledged. Psychiatric or psychology referral to be placed if mood symptoms worsen or meet criteria for major depressive disorder at next assessment.

11. GERMLINE BRCA1 STATUS AND FUTURE CONSIDERATIONS:
Patient has confirmed pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC). Genetic counseling was completed prior to treatment. Surgical planning will include formal discussion with breast surgery regarding bilateral mastectomy options (including contralateral prophylactic mastectomy given germline status). Depending on pathologic response, adjuvant ola (per OlympiA trial data) and adjuvant Xeloda (per CREATE-X, for residual disease) will be considerations at the time of post-operative planning.
    - Plan: Breast surgery referral confirmed and consultation appointment scheduled for approximately week 18 (after completion of all neoadjuvant therapy). Will revisit PARP inhibitor and adjuvant cape discussions at that time based on pathologic response.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
Cycle 3 of ddAC/Keytruda today. Patient to return in 2 weeks for cycle 4 pre-treatment assessment. Mid-neoadjuvant breast MRI to be scheduled approximately 2-3 weeks after cycle 4 administration. Urgency precautions reviewed: patient instructed to call or present to the emergency department for fever > 38.3°C, intractable nausea/vomiting, severe diarrhea, chest pain, dyspnea, or any new neurologic symptoms.

Time spent with patient: Approximately 55 minutes (including counseling, examination, and coordination of care).
